claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin. To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your child.

**First question: How old is your child?**

This will help me determine the right path for your assessment:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (subject to other conditions)
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over are not eligible

Please tell me your child's age.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I actually have three children I'd like to ask about - Alex who's 5, Blake who's 17, and Charlie who's 19. Should I go through them one at a time, or can we check all three?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hi! Thanks for providing that information. Yes, we can definitely check all three of your children - Alex (5), Blake (17), and Charlie (19). It will be most efficient to go through them one at a time since each child may have different eligibility criteria depending on their age.

Let me start with **Alex (age 5)**:

Since Alex is under 16, that's a good starting point. Let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (age 5):**

Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)? 

(I'm assuming a 5-year-old wouldn't be working, but I need to confirm this to continue through the assessment properly!)

Once we finish Alex's assessment, we'll move on to Blake (17) and then Charlie (19). Blake and Charlie will have additional questions about education or training since they're in the 16-19 age range.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Ha, no, Alex is only 5 - definitely not working! He's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That makes sense. Let me continue with Alex's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (age 5):**

Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?

(Again, for a 5-year-old this would be very unlikely, but I need to confirm to continue the assessment.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name - he's just 5!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue with Alex's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (age 5):**

Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship program?

(Once again, for a 5-year-old this wouldn't apply, but I need to confirm this step!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in an apprenticeship - he's only 5, still in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now for the claimant requirements (this applies to you):**

Do you meet the UK residency requirements? You qualify if **ANY** of the following applies to you:

1. You normally live in the UK
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources (not claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit)
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do you meet any of these residency requirements?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (age 5):**

Are you responsible for Alex? You qualify if **ANY** of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex**
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** - paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care, including things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement that equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

**Do any of these apply to you for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (age 5):**

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex (for example, another parent or guardian)?

- If no one else is claiming, we'll continue
- If someone else is claiming but you've agreed to transfer it to you, we can continue
- If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC will need to decide

**What's the situation for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with Alex's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (age 5):**

I need to check Alex's living situation. Which of these applies to Alex?

1. **In hospital** - Is Alex currently in hospital?
2. **In care** - Is Alex in local authority care?
3. **Fostered** - Is Alex in a foster care arrangement?
4. **Living normally** - Is Alex living at home with you normally?

**Which situation applies to Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living at home with me normally - just a regular situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (age 5):**

Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

In other words, are you paying for Alex's living costs yourself (not the council)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm paying for everything myself - the council isn't paying anything towards Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me check the outcome for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Great news for Alex (age 5)! ✓ ELIGIBLE**

Alex meets all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements!

---

Now let's move on to **Blake (age 17)**. Since Blake is in the 16-19 age range, there are additional requirements about education or training.

Let me start Blake's assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (age 17):**

Is Blake currently:
1. **In approved education or training**, OR
2. **Recently left education** (within the last 20 weeks), OR
3. **Neither** (not in education/training and didn't recently leave)

**Which situation applies to Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake isn't in education or training anymore - he left school and hasn't been in any education or training since.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (age 17):**

Let me clarify - when did Blake leave education? 

- If it was **within the last 20 weeks**, Blake may still be eligible for an extension period
- If it was **more than 20 weeks ago**, Blake would not be eligible

**How long ago did Blake leave education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been more than 20 weeks - quite a while ago now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I see. Let me process that for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (age 17): ✗ NOT ELIGIBLE**

Unfortunately, Blake does not qualify as a qualifying child because:
- Blake is 16-19 years old
- Blake is not currently in approved education or training
- Blake left education more than 20 weeks ago (the extension period has expired)

**Note:** If Blake returns to approved education or training in the future, you could become eligible for Child Benefit for Blake again.

---

Now let's check **Charlie (age 19)**. Since Charlie is also in the 16-19 age range, similar education/training requirements will apply.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (age 19):**

Is Charlie currently:
1. **In approved education or training**, OR
2. **Recently left education** (within the last 20 weeks), OR
3. **Neither** (not in education/training and didn't recently leave)

**Which situation applies to Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Charlie is in education - doing A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue with Charlie's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (age 19):**

Charlie is doing A-levels, which is approved non-advanced education. Now I need to check a few more details:

1. **Is Charlie studying full-time** (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)?
2. **Is this education funded by an employer** or part of an agreement with an employer for a future job?

**Can you confirm these details for Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie is studying full-time - it's a proper full-time A-level course at college. And no, it's not funded by an employer or anything like that - just regular college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with Charlie's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (age 19):**

Is Charlie working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?

Children aged 16-19 in approved education can still work part-time, but if they're working 24 hours or more per week, they don't qualify for Child Benefit.

**Is Charlie working less than 24 hours per week?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie isn't working at all - just focusing on the A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (age 19):**

Is Charlie NOT receiving benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?

**Does Charlie receive any benefits in their own name?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (age 19):**

Is Charlie in any type of apprenticeship program? (You mentioned Charlie is doing A-levels at sixth form college, so I'm assuming not, but I need to confirm.)

**Is Charlie in an apprenticeship?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie isn't in an apprenticeship - just doing A-levels at college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with Charlie's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (age 19):**

We already confirmed earlier that you live in Manchester, UK, so the residency requirement is met. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (age 19):**

Does Charlie live at home with you in Manchester?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (age 19):**

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (age 19):**

Is Charlie living at home with you normally, or is Charlie currently in hospital, in local authority care, or in foster care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Charlie is living at home with me normally - just a regular situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (age 19):**

Is the local council NOT paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance? In other words, are you paying for Charlie's living costs yourself (not the council)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm paying for everything myself - the council isn't paying anything towards Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me check the outcome for Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Great news for Charlie (age 19)! ✓ ELIGIBLE**

Charlie meets all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements!

---

## **Summary of Your Child Benefit Eligibility:**

Let me now provide you with the complete assessment for all three children:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

### **📋 FINAL ELIGIBILITY ASSESSMENT**

**✓ Alex (age 5): ELIGIBLE**
- As a child under 16, Alex qualifies automatically
- All other requirements are met

**✗ Blake (age 17): NOT ELIGIBLE** 
- Blake is not in education or training
- Left education more than 20 weeks ago (extension period expired)
- Note: If Blake returns to approved education or training, eligibility could be restored

**✓ Charlie (age 19): ELIGIBLE**
- In approved full-time education (A-levels)
- Meets all requirements for 16-19 year olds in education

---

### **📝 Next Steps for Alex and Charlie:**

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and the children
3. **Provide birth certificates** for Alex and Charlie
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - if you or your partner earn over £60,000, you may have to pay back some or all of the benefit through the tax system
5. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### **💡 Important Note about Blake:**

If Blake enrolls in approved education or training in the future (such as A-levels, T-levels, NVQs Level 3 or below, etc.), you could become eligible for Child Benefit for Blake again. The education must be:
- Non-advanced (not university level)
- Full-time (12+ hours per week of supervised study)
- Not funded by an employer

You can find more information and apply at: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about your Child Benefit eligibility?

reasonings: Alex (age 5) is eligible for Child Benefit. As a child under 16, Alex automatically qualifies as a qualifying child. Alex is not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with you in the UK, you are responsible for Alex, no one else is claiming for Alex, Alex is living normally at home, and the council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake (age 17) is NOT eligible for Child Benefit. Blake is in the 16-19 age range, which requires being in approved education or training to qualify as a qualifying child. Blake is not currently in education or training and left education more than 20 weeks ago, which means Blake does not meet the qualifying child criteria and the extension period has expired. Without being in approved education or training, Blake cannot qualify for Child Benefit.

Charlie (age 19) is eligible for Child Benefit. Charlie is in the 16-19 age range and is in approved non-advanced education (full-time A-levels at sixth form college, studying 12+ hours per week). Charlie is not working 24+ hours per week, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with you in the UK, you are responsible for Charlie, no one else is claiming for Charlie, Charlie is living normally at home, and the council is not paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for two of your three children: Alex (age 5) and Charlie (age 19). Alex qualifies as a child under 16, and Charlie qualifies as a 16-19 year old in approved full-time education (A-levels). Blake (age 17) does not currently qualify because Blake is not in education or training and left education more than 20 weeks ago. You meet all the claimant requirements (UK residency, responsibility for the children, and no other claimants).

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.reason: No qualifying child under 16, or under 20 in approved education/training
response.guidance: Child must be under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. If child just turned 20, benefit stops. If 16-17 year old leaves education, benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with careers service or armed services.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Payments cover the child's upkeep
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 5) is eligible for Child Benefit. As a child under 16, Alex automatically qualifies as a qualifying child. Alex is not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with you in the UK, you are responsible for Alex, no one else is claiming for Alex, Alex is living normally at home, and the council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake (age 17) is NOT eligible for Child Benefit. Blake is in the 16-19 age range, which requires being in approved education or training to qualify as a qualifying child. Blake is not currently in education or training and left education more than 20 weeks ago, which means Blake does not meet the qualifying child criteria and the extension period has expired. Without being in approved education or training, Blake cannot qualify for Child Benefit.

Charlie (age 19) is eligible for Child Benefit. Charlie is in the 16-19 age range and is in approved non-advanced education (full-time A-levels at sixth form college, studying 12+ hours per week). Charlie is not working 24+ hours per week, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with you in the UK, you are responsible for Charlie, no one else is claiming for Charlie, Charlie is living normally at home, and the council is not paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for two of your three children: Alex (age 5) and Charlie (age 19). Alex qualifies as a child under 16, and Charlie qualifies as a 16-19 year old in approved full-time education (A-levels). Blake (age 17) does not currently qualify because Blake is not in education or training and left education more than 20 weeks ago. You meet all the claimant requirements (UK residency, responsibility for the children, and no other claimants).
